title: Integrated time-bin entangled quantum light source on a 4H-SiC microring chip
authors: Hong Zeng, Bing-Cheng Yang, Yun-Ru Fan, Li-Ping Zhou, Cheng-Li Wang, Bo-Wen Chen, Ai-Lun Yi, Yong Geng, Guang-Wei Deng, You Wang, Hai-Zhi Song, Jun-Tao Zhang, Hao Li, Li-Xing You, Zi-Hao Zhan, Kai Guo, Xin Ou, Guang-Can Guo, Qiang Zhou
abstract: Integrated time-bin-entangled photon-pair source with cavity-enhanced nonlinear optical processes is essential for quantum information technologies. However, microcavities with a high quality factor inherently introduce a trade-off between generation efficiency and photon bandwidth, which hinders the development of high-speed quantum networks with an integrated source. Here, we address this challenge by optimizing the nonlinearity property of the material and the geometry of the integrated microring resonator with a 4H-silicon carbide platform. Operating at a loaded quality factor of 1.9 $\times$ 10^5 - spectral bandwidth of 1.0 GHz and pumped with 300-ps double pulses separated by 1.25 ns at a repetition rate of 160 MHz, the device achieves a time-bin-entangled photon-pair generation rate of 1.35 $\times$ 10^7 s^-1 mW^-2. A raw visibility of 95.55 $\pm$ 0.18% is measured, showing a violation of Bell's inequality by more than 138 standard deviations, and a fidelity of 94.37 $\pm$ 0.22% is obtained by quantum state tomography. These results provide a scalable pathway to an efficient and broadband time-bin entangled quantum light source, overcoming intrinsic limitations of cavity-based designs and advancing integrated platforms for future quantum communication networks.
summary: This paper demonstrates an integrated 4H-SiC microring resonator that overcomes the traditional trade-off between photon generation efficiency and spectral bandwidth. By optimizing the material's nonlinearity and the resonator's geometry, the authors achieved high-fidelity time-bin entanglement suitable for high-speed quantum networks. This provides a scalable pathway for developing efficient, broadband quantum light sources on a chip.
main_problem: The efficiency-bandwidth trade-off in cavity-enhanced quantum light sources, where high quality factors increase generation efficiency but narrow the spectral bandwidth, limiting high-speed quantum communication.
main_result: The researchers developed an integrated 4H-SiC microring resonator that achieves high photon-pair generation rates and a 1.0 GHz bandwidth, demonstrating time-bin entanglement with 94.37% fidelity and a Bell inequality violation of over 138 standard deviations.
method: The study uses spontaneous four-wave mixing (SFWM) in a 4H-SiC microring resonator, optimizing material nonlinearity and device geometry to decouple efficiency from bandwidth, and characterizes entanglement via quantum state tomography and unbalanced Mach-Zehnder interferometry.
model_or_system: An integrated 4H-silicon carbide (4H-SiC) microring resonator (MRR) on a photonic integrated circuit, utilizing a 1555.75 nm pulsed pump to generate time-bin entangled photon pairs via third-order nonlinearity.
key_observables: Photon-pair generation rate (PGR), coincidence-to-accidental ratio (CAR), interference visibility, Bell inequality violation (CHSH), and quantum state tomography fidelity.
important_parameters: Loaded Q-factors of 1.1-1.9 x 10^5, spectral bandwidth of 1.0-1.7 GHz, photon-pair generation rate of 1.35 x 10^7 s^-1 mW^-2, and a 160 MHz repetition rate.
figures_summary: The figures likely illustrate the device geometry, the experimental setup for photon generation and detection, the scaling of photon-pair generation with pump power, and the reconstruction of the density matrix and interference fringes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the efficiency-bandwidth trade-off, describes the 4H-SiC microring resonator platform and optimization strategy, details the experimental setup and photon generation process, presents the characterization of entanglement and generation rates, and concludes with the implications for scalable quantum networks.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information because it presents a scalable, integrated hardware solution for generating high-rate, broadband entangled photons, which is a critical component for high-speed quantum communication and networking.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 2. Experimental setup for the generation and characterization of integrated time- bin entangled photon pairs. (a) Generation of time-bin entangled photon pairs with plused-laser pump. (b) Correlation property. (c) Time-bin entanglement. CW laser: continuous wave laser, IM: intensity modulator, EDFA: erbium-doped fiber amplifier, AWG: arbitrary waveform generator, VOA: variable optical attenuator, PC: polarization controller, BS: beam splitter, PM: power meter, DWDM: dense wavelength-division multiplexer, SNSPD: superconducting nanowire single-photon detector, TDC: time- to-digital converter, UMZI: unbalanced Mach–Zehnder interferometer. 𝜆𝑠and 𝜆𝑖are wavelengths of signal and idler...

Fig. 3. Correlated photon pair generation in SiC microring. (a) Single side count rate of the idler photon versus pump power. (b) The left shows the coincidence and accidental coincidence count rate versus pump power. The right is the measured coincidence histogram of the signal and idler photons at ITU-C37 and ITU-C17 under 0.29 mW. (c) Coincidence-to-accidental ratio (CAR) and PGR versus pump power.

Fig. 4. Characterization of time-bin entanglement. (a) and (b) Interference fringes as a function of the relative phase. The coincidences are measured between the synchronous electrical signal and photon detection events at four different output port combinations: A1&B1, A1&B2, A2&B1, and A2&B2. (c) Correlation coefficient. (d) Counts of the four UMZI ports and typical post-selection results based on triple coincidence. (e) Real and (f) imaginary parts of the reconstructed density matrix 𝜌for the time-bin entangled state.
